Q1: I had what I thought was a decent routine going - I'd set aside time a few days a week to log in and check what needed to be done. I'd usually skim through the materials and make notes in my notebook, which is how I prefer to study. But honestly, looking back, I wasn't putting in as much focused effort as I should have. I'd go through the motions of logging in and clicking around, but I wasn't always deeply processing what I was reading.
Q2: My pattern was pretty regular actually - I tried to stick to certain days and times each week. I'm the kind of person who likes having a schedule, even if I don't always follow through completely. Deadlines didn't change my routine much because I was already logging in regularly, though I'll admit the quality of my engagement would drop when other things came up with my extracurriculars or life stuff.
Q3: Honestly, it was just habit more than anything specific. I'd log in because it felt like the responsible thing to do, like checking off a box. Sometimes I'd get a notification or remember an assignment was coming up, but mostly it was just part of my weekly routine. I'd tell myself I was staying on top of things even when I wasn't really engaging deeply.
Q4: Yeah, logging in definitely became automatic for me pretty early on. I set it up as part of my morning routine a few days a week - grab coffee, check the LMS, see what's new. The problem was that it became so routine that I wasn't really thinking about what I was doing. I'd browse around, maybe watch part of a video, and then move on feeling like I'd accomplished something when I really hadn't.
Q5: The reading materials were most useful to me since that's how I learn best - I'd read through the course content and take notes by hand. The discussion posts from other students sometimes gave me different perspectives I hadn't considered. I appreciated when things were clearly laid out in text form rather than just video content, because I could go at my own pace and highlight key points.
Q6: I mostly skipped the longer videos or would start them and not finish. Watching a 40-minute lecture just didn't hold my attention the way reading did. I'd also ignore a lot of the optional supplementary materials because I figured I was already doing enough by keeping up with the main content. Looking back, maybe those could have helped me understand things better for the exams.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or the exam, it felt worth my time. I also valued activities that I could complete relatively quickly and check off my list. The mindfulness exercises they introduced were actually surprisingly helpful for my stress levels, even though they didn't directly relate to course content. I liked feeling productive without being overwhelmed.
Q8: Required activities felt like obligations I needed to get through, while optional ones felt like extras I could skip without consequences. I rarely did optional activities unless I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which wasn't often. There was less pressure with optional stuff, but that also meant less motivation to actually do them. I guess I prioritized what I thought would directly impact my grade.
Q9: Definitely - I'd download the PDFs and reading materials and study from those on my laptop without being connected to the LMS. I also used my handwritten notes extensively, which is really how I process information best. Sometimes I'd look things up on external websites to clarify concepts that confused me. A lot of my actual studying happened away from the platform.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd spend an hour reviewing my notes or re-reading downloaded materials, and none of that would show up anywhere. I'd also discuss course concepts with friends in my extracurricular activities who were taking similar classes. My actual study time was probably more than it looked like online, though I'll admit my exam score suggests I wasn't studying effectively regardless.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd open a PDF once on the LMS, download it, and then never open it through the platform again. I preferred having everything saved locally so I could access it without internet or distractions. The LMS would only show that initial access, but I might have reviewed that same document multiple times on my own computer over the following weeks.
Q12: Around midterms, everything just felt harder to manage. I had commitments with my extracurriculars that were demanding more time, and I started feeling disconnected from the course material. It wasn't that I was stressed exactly - I just felt kind of flat about everything. The mindfulness intervention helped a bit, but I was surprised by how much I'd fallen behind without really noticing.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected things. When my extracurriculars got busy, the LMS became more of a quick check-in rather than actual engagement. I wasn't particularly stressed or fatigued, but I also wasn't prioritizing the coursework the way I should have. I'd convince myself that my routine logins meant I was keeping up, but I wasn't actually completing assignments or preparing properly.
Q14: Oh, for sure. I'd log in, scroll through some content, maybe watch a few minutes of a video, and then realize I hadn't absorbed anything. My mind would wander to other things I needed to do. It was like going through the motions without actually being present. I'd close the browser feeling like I'd done something productive when really I'd just been clicking around.
Q15: The assignments especially - I'd know they were due and tell myself I'd get to them, but then I just... didn't. I think part of it was that I didn't feel urgency because my stress was low and everything seemed manageable. By the time I realized how far behind I was on completion, it felt overwhelming to catch up. I kept thinking I had more time than I actually did.
Q16: Deadlines should have motivated me more than they did. I'd see them approaching and think about starting, but my low-key attitude meant I didn't feel that panic that usually pushes people to finish. I'd log in more frequently near deadlines, but that didn't translate to actually submitting work. The deadline would pass and I'd feel surprised, then just move on to the next thing.
Q17: When I did get feedback, it was usually too late for me to really incorporate it into my approach. I appreciated the comments, but by then I'd already moved on mentally. The lack of regular feedback on my incomplete assignments meant I didn't have a clear picture of how poorly I was doing. If I'd gotten more immediate responses, maybe I would have adjusted sooner.
Q18: The course structure was pretty standard - weekly modules, assignments, discussions. Having that predictable format helped me maintain my routine of logging in regularly. But the structure didn't force me to actually complete things, so I could coast along feeling engaged without producing much. More mandatory checkpoints might have kept me more accountable.
Q19: The flexibility made it easy to fall behind without consequences until it was too late. I appreciated being able to access materials anytime, but that also meant I could always tell myself I'd do it later. Having more text-based content helped my learning style, but the lack of structured accountability made it too easy to just browse rather than complete work.
Q20: That's basically me, isn't it? I think it happens when someone is going through the motions of being a student without actually committing to the work. You can spend a lot of time clicking around, reading bits and pieces, feeling like you're engaged, but never actually producing anything. It's a weird disconnect between activity and output that I didn't fully recognize until I failed.
Q21: I could see that happening for students who are really efficient or who do most of their work offline. Some people can read something once, understand it, and perform well without needing to interact with the platform much. That wasn't really my experience though - I had high activity but clearly wasn't learning effectively, so maybe I'm the opposite example.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when and how often someone logs in, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. But they can't show whether someone is actually thinking about the material or just staring at the screen. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with peers, the note-taking. They definitely can't capture whether someone is mentally present or just going through motions.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about and investing in what you're learning - not just showing up, but being mentally present and putting in effort. It means completing work, asking questions when confused, and trying to understand rather than just get through. Looking at my own experience, I was present in the system but not really engaged in the learning. There's a difference between routine compliance and genuine engagement.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how disconnected I felt from the material even while maintaining my routine. It wouldn't capture that I was surprised by my own failure because I thought I was doing okay. It can't show that I actually did some studying offline, just not effectively. And it definitely can't show that despite failing, I wasn't particularly stressed about it, which is maybe part of the problem.
